item.title: 1/2 How often is it suggested that in British urban centres, and especially in L...
item.description:
@stchadsprojects

1/2 How often is it suggested that in British urban centres, and especially in London, you're never more than six feet away from a rat?
It’s a myth, of course (it’s more like 164 feet) but stories persist across generations and centuries. At the moment there is a very big rat in a former hospital storage room near King’s Cross. It’s asleep and it’s golden. It is also inflatable and the work of Danish artist Esben Weile Kjær.
Installed at the constantly interesting St Chads Project Space, the piece is visually spectacular, filling the space so that any visitor comes into close proximity with the resident – and much closer than the fabled six feet.
The strength of the vast object, called Lion, lies beyond the theatrical thrill of its size, colour and unexpectedness, potent though they are on first meeting. But you have to dig for the resonances Kjær has folded into this event.
Because converging on Lion are a number of lines of thought that inflate the significance of even this mighty mini-presence. The rat has acquired potent symbolic meaning in recent decades, not least in Denmark where its image is used as emblems by punks, gays and the political far-right. It appears on posters and flags, and can be seen at demonstrations organised by counter-cultural groups.
That detail feeds into Kjær’s formative past, when he lived in squats as a young man in Copenhagen and was active in his contemporaries’ protests at the rise of right-wing extremist groups among, for instance, football fans.
But that doesn’t account for this rat’s recumbent slumber. Staying with Denmark, Kjær read about a plague of rats under Rosenborg Castle, the renaissance palace where the country’s crown jewels are stored.
Rats and royals: that appealed to the artist’s rebellious sensibilities. He made a connection with another subterranean presence, this time at another royal residence, Kronborg Castle (where Shakespeare set “Hamlet”). In an underground chamber is the Danish "sleeping giant” Holger Danske (Ogier the Dane). Since the 13th century, the story goes that this warrior from the time of Charlemagne will awaken to defend Denmark when the nation is in mortal danger. To be continued...
item.content:encoded:
@stchadsprojects

1/2 How often is it suggested that in British urban centres, and especially in London, you're never more than six feet away from a rat?
It’s a myth, of course (it’s more like 164 feet) but stories persist across generations and centuries. At the moment there is a very big rat in a former hospital storage room near King’s Cross. It’s asleep and it’s golden. It is also inflatable and the work of Danish artist Esben Weile Kjær.
Installed at the constantly interesting St Chads Project Space, the piece is visually spectacular, filling the space so that any visitor comes into close proximity with the resident – and much closer than the fabled six feet.
The strength of the vast object, called Lion, lies beyond the theatrical thrill of its size, colour and unexpectedness, potent though they are on first meeting. But you have to dig for the resonances Kjær has folded into this event.
Because converging on Lion are a number of lines of thought that inflate the significance of even this mighty mini-presence. The rat has acquired potent symbolic meaning in recent decades, not least in Denmark where its image is used as emblems by punks, gays and the political far-right. It appears on posters and flags, and can be seen at demonstrations organised by counter-cultural groups.
That detail feeds into Kjær’s formative past, when he lived in squats as a young man in Copenhagen and was active in his contemporaries’ protests at the rise of right-wing extremist groups among, for instance, football fans.
But that doesn’t account for this rat’s recumbent slumber. Staying with Denmark, Kjær read about a plague of rats under Rosenborg Castle, the renaissance palace where the country’s crown jewels are stored.
Rats and royals: that appealed to the artist’s rebellious sensibilities. He made a connection with another subterranean presence, this time at another royal residence, Kronborg Castle (where Shakespeare set “Hamlet”). In an underground chamber is the Danish "sleeping giant” Holger Danske (Ogier the Dane). Since the 13th century, the story goes that this warrior from the time of Charlemagne will awaken to defend Denmark when the nation is in mortal danger. To be continued...
item.author: stchadsprojects

item.title: Lion Fabric, motor 2025 By Esben Weile Kjaer @esbenweile Lions Open by appointme...
item.description:
@stchadsprojects

Lion
Fabric, motor
2025

By Esben Weile Kjaer
@esbenweile

Lions
Open by appointment
15.2-30.5.2026

At St. Chads
@stchadsprojects

Curated by Benjamin Orlow
@benjaminorlow

Photography by @studio_adamson

Massive thanks to @laurel_mccallum_

Accessibility: the space is wheelchair accessible
